6: ho de gramma apoktennei to de pneuma zoopoiei (ho de gramma apoktennei, to de pneuma zōopoiei): 'the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life.' Gramma (letter/writing) refers not to the literal-vs-spiritual sense of Scripture but to the Mosaic covenant in its external, inscribed form — the same covenant Paul calls a 'ministry of death' (diakonia tou thanatou, v. 7) not because Torah is evil (cf. Rom 7:12: the law is holy, righteous, and good) but because it cannot provide the Spirit that enables fulfillment. The contrast is old covenant (palaia diatheke, v. 14) vs new covenant (kaine diatheke, v. 6), not text vs allegory.
18: ten auten eikona metamorphoumetha apo doxes eis doxan (tēn autēn eikona metamorphoumetha apo doxēs eis doxan): 'we are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another.' Metamorphoō (transform/transfigure) is the same word used for the Transfiguration (Matt 17:2; Mark 9:2) and Rom 12:2 (do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind). The transformation is: (1) into the image of Christ (eikon); (2) progressive (from glory to glory); (3) mediated by the Spirit (apo Kyriou Pneumatos). The eschatological glorification (Rom 8:30) is already underway in the present — theosis-language without the ontological merger of later traditions.
1: Archometha palin heautous synistanein: are we beginning again to commend ourselves? The opponents apparently arrived with epistolai systatikai (letters of recommendation) — formal credentials from other communities or apostles. Paul must explain why he needs no such letters without appearing to be merely self-promoting.
2: He epistole hemon hymeis este: you are our letter. Engegramene en tais kardiais hemon: written in our hearts (variant: 'your hearts' — MSS differ). MKT follows the 'our hearts' reading. Ginoskomene kai anaginoskomene hypo panton anthropon: known and read by all people. The community's transformed life is publicly readable testimony — more persuasive than any papyrus recommendation.
3: Phaneroymenoi hoti este epistole Christou: manifestly a letter of Christ (Christou — genitive of authorship; Christ authored it). Diakonettheisa hyph' hemon: administered/delivered by us — Paul is the mail-carrier, not the author. Pneumati theou zontos: written with the Spirit of the living God. Ouk en plaxin lithinais alla en plaxin kardiais sarkinais: not on stone tablets but on tablets of fleshly hearts — Jer 31:33/Ezek 36:26 new-covenant fulfillment subtext.
4: Pepoithesis de toiauten echomen dia tou Christou pros ton theon: such confidence (pepoithesis — settled trust, firm persuasion) through Christ toward God. The confidence is not in Paul's performance but in the Christ-mediated relationship with God — setting up the competence argument: confidence is given, not self-generated.
5: Ouch hoti aph' heauton hikanoi esmen logisasthai ti hos ex heauton: not that we are sufficient (hikanos) of ourselves to reckon anything as from ourselves. This answers 2:16's question 'who is sufficient?' All' he hikaniotes hemon ek tou theou: our sufficiency is from God. The ek theou entirely replaces the aph' heauton — divine source, not human resource.
7: Ei de he diakonia tou thanatou en grammasin entetypomene lithou egeneto en doxe: if the ministry of death (diakonia tou thanatou), engraved in letters on stone, came in glory so that Israel could not gaze at Moses' face (Exod 34:29-35). The argument from Sinai's glory: even the old covenant had genuine divine glory, so much more the new. Grammasin: the written letters on stone — the same root as the letter (gramma) that kills (v.6).
8: Pos ouchi mallon he diakonia tou pneumatos estai en doxe: how much more will the ministry of the Spirit be in glory? The a fortiori argument: lesser to greater, old covenant glory to new covenant glory. Diakonia tou pneumatos: ministry characterized by the Spirit, not by written code.
9: Ei gar te diakonia tes katakrises doxa, pollo mallon perisseuei he diakonia tes dikaiosyne en doxe: if the ministry of condemnation (katakrisis) had glory, much more does the ministry of righteousness (dikaiosyne, dispute level 2) abound in glory. Dikaiosyne here is the judicial right-standing that the new covenant conveys — the forensic declaration of rightness before God, not merely ethical uprightness.
10: Kai gar ou dedoxastai to dedoxasmenon en touto to merei heineka tes hyperballoystes doxes: what was glorious appears inglorious by comparison because of the surpassing glory. The comparison is quantitative, not qualitative negation — the old covenant was genuinely glorious; the new so far exceeds it that the old appears inglorious by contrast.
11: Ei gar to katargoumenon dia doxes, pollo mallon to menon en doxe: if what is being abolished (katargeo — the key Pauline verb for rendering ineffective; used for powers, laws, and death in 1 Cor 15:24-26) came through glory, much more what remains (meno) is in glory. The permanence/temporary contrast grounds the new covenant's superiority.
12: Echontes oun toiauten elpida, polle parresia chrometha: having such hope (elpis, dispute level 2) we use great boldness (parresia — frank speech, freedom of speech; Athenian democratic ideal; in Jewish contexts, access before God). The permanence of the new covenant (v.11) grounds Paul's confident, unguarded speech.
13: Kai ou katha per Mousas etithe kalymma epi to prosopon autou: not like Moses who put a veil on his face to prevent Israel from seeing the end (telos) of the fading. To katargoumenon: the fading/abolishing thing. MKT 'to prevent the Israelites from seeing the end of what was passing away' captures the Pauline reading. This is a midrashic interpretation of Exod 34 — Paul reads the veil as concealing the temporary nature of the Mosaic glory.
14: Alla eporo the ta noemata auton: their minds were hardened (poroo — hardened as with a callus). To auto kalymma epi te anagnosei tes palaias diathekes menei akalypton: the same veil remains unlifted over the reading of the old covenant. Hoti en Christo katargeitai: because in Christ it is being abolished. The Christological key to scriptural understanding: the veil over the mind lifts only in Christ.
15: All' heos semeron henika an anaginosketai Mousas, kalymma epi ten kardian auton keitai: to this day when Moses is read, a veil lies over their hearts. The veil has migrated from Moses' face (Exod 34) to the readers' hearts — Paul's application. The problem is not the text but the hearts that read it without Christ (cf. 1 Cor 2:14).
16: Heneka de ean epistrepse pros kyrion, periaireitai to kalymma: whenever anyone turns to the Lord (epistrepho — the conversion verb), the veil is removed. Paul reads Exod 34:34 (Moses entered the Lord's presence and removed the veil) as a type of conversion: turning to Christ removes the interpretive veil. The 'Lord' of Exod 34 is applied to Christ — a Christological exegesis of the Exodus text.
17: Ho de Kyrios to pneuma estin: the Lord is the Spirit. One of Paul's most contested identifications. The 'Lord' of v.16 (Exod 34:34) is identified with the Spirit. MKT renders literally, preserving the identification without resolving whether ontological (Christ and Spirit are numerically one) or functional (encounter with the Spirit is encounter with the Lord). Hou de to pneuma Kyriou, eleutheria: where the Spirit of the Lord is, freedom (eleutheria) — from the veil, from condemnation, from the letter that kills.
